title: Lab to Life: Why I'm Taking My Thesis Outside
body:
When I presented my original plan at the colloquium in January, the faculty gently redirected me. My designed lab experiment was sound, but they asked: *Can you actually build this? Will it get ethical approval? And who will it help?* That question changed everything.

I'd been focused on proving a quick, controlled effect. Instead, I've pivoted to something bigger: understanding whether coordinating breath and movement is a *learnable skill* that people can develop over weeks and months of real practice — outside a lab, in their actual lives.

The shift unlocked a new approach. Rather than measuring acute responses, I'm now tracking how people's heart-rate variability (a marker of nervous system flexibility) evolves as they practice. My collaborators and I built Loob, a simple motion tracker that works with just a webcam, and we're partnering with 8OS, a peer project whose HRV software aligns perfectly with the physiology I'm investigating.


I'm moving to running two tiers: a proof-of-concept starting this month with just Apple Watches, and a larger study if funding comes through. For the first time, this feels like something that could actually work — and matter.

title: From Brain to Hearts: How my Thesis Found Its Rhythm
body:
When I (re)started this journey in July 2025, I was deep in neuroscience papers trying to build a theory about how a particular brain receptor might explain altered states of consciousness. It felt ambitious and distant from anything I could actually test—and I was running out of time. My visa expires at the end of September, and my degree depends on it.

Then something unexpected happened. I found a way to integrate my underground research into my thesis work in cognitive neuroscience: the ⦿-⦿. It's simple, tactile, and there's already a community of about 33 people using it informally. That opened a door.

My focus shifted from abstract brain chemistry to something I could measure: what happens to people's hearts when they move with rhythm? Specifically, I'm now asking whether spinning the ⦿-⦿ in time with music can synchronize someone's heart rate and breathing in measurable ways. It sounds niche, but it touches on something real—how our bodies entrain to external rhythms.

The design began coming into focus: I'll test whether the stick helps people sync their physiology to music, whether it matters if they're moving *with* or *against* their natural rhythm, and whether I can capture these changes using affordable heart-rate sensors.

I have a willing participant pool right here in Berlin. The hypothesis object has shifted from emotion to physiology, from theory to data. And honestly? That feels like progress.

title: From Fidgeting to Breathing: How a Spinning Stick Became a Window into Physiology
body:
When I started this project, I was looking into e-motions; trying to understand how rhythmic fidgeting might calm us down. But somewhere along the way, my research found its heartbeat.

The turning point came when I discovered **locomotor–respiratory coupling (LRC)**: the scientific principle that rhythmic movement and breathing naturally synchronise. It's well-documented for walking and cycling, but nobody had tested it with something simple—like spinning a stick. That became my question: can a gentle, rhythmic movement entrain your breathing without needing to work hard physically?

This reframe felt like finding the missing puzzle piece. Instead of measuring emotions (which are slippery), I'm now measuring heart-rate variability and respiratory patterns during and after spinning—clean, quantifiable signals that reveal what's actually happening in your body.

My design crystallised: a controlled lab study with about ten people, each doing four conditions (spinning with sound, spinning in silence, sitting still with sound, sitting still in silence). I'm tracking physiological recovery—how well their nervous system settles afterwards.

The real test comes in January. I'm presenting this at a research colloquium, then launching the first experiments by spring. What excites me most isn't the fidgeting anymore,it's proving that something so simple can genuinely shift how our bodies work.
